coredata.dc:title: The carbon dots as colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout probe for 2-nitrophenol and 4-nitrophenol detection
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, YaoYao
Ren, Guojuan
Yu, Liying
Zhu, Baoya
Chai, Fang
Chen, Lihua
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has been synthesized by hydrothermal method by using ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a raw materials. The prepared CDs exhibited bright blue fluorscence excited by 340 nm with the quantum yield as high as 52%. The CDs possess special fluorescence responses to 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), and the slump of fluorescence was demonstrated by CDs solution in the case of 2-nitrophenol and 4-nitrophenol existence. The detection limit of 2-NP and 4-NP was calculated at 77 nM and 60 nM, respectively. Meanwhile, the color response to 2-NP and 4-NP can be observed from achromatic solution to yellow by naked eye. All in all, the obtained CDs can be applied as fluorescence and colorimetric probe in environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-readout probe
Detection
2-nitrophenol
4-nitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensing for 2-NP and 4-NP
Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
The selectivity of the CDs for 2-NP and 4-NP detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Fund ( LBH-Q17084 ), Higher Education Teaching Reform Research of Heilongjiang Province ( SJGY20170179 ) and Qingdao University of Science and Technology ( QUSTHX201801 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs (Inset: high resolution TEM image of CDs and size distribution of the CDs). (B) FT-IR spectrum of the as-prepared CDs.
(A) XPS full spectrum measurement of CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) O1s and (D) N1s.

(A) UV–vis absorption and FL emission spectra for the CDs. The insets display the corresponding optical photographs of the CDs solution. (B) FL emission spectra of CDs with variations of excitation wavelength. (C) Fluorescence decay curve of the CDs.
(A) Dependent fluorescent intensity ratio (I/I0) of the CDs with the different condition of high NaCl concentration, (B) storage time and (C) pH.

Selectivity of CDs: (A) Fluorescence emission of CDs with 100 μM of nitroaromatic compounds. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs in presence of 100 μM of different nitroaromatic compounds. (C) Corresponding photographs of the samples under 365 nm UV light and (D) daylight (from left to right, Blank, 4-NP, 2-NP, TNT, TNP, 3-NP, DNT, 3-NT, NB and toluene).

(A), (C) Interference tests of other nitroaromatic compounds on relative FL peak intensities of CDs (concentration of all sample was 1 mM). (B), (D) The corresponding photo images under the UV lamp and daylight.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs on addition different of concentrations of 2-NP solution (0–0.001 μM). Inset: the linear plots of I/I0 versus the 2-NP concentrations in the range of 0.001–1 μM (R2 =0.98). (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs at different concentrations of 2-NP solution. The corresponding photo images of detection (C) under UV lamp and (D) daylight.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence different concentrations of 4-NP. Inset: plots of the values of I/I0 at 340 nm versus the concentration of 4-NP (R2 =0.99). (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs in presence of 4-NP solution. Photographs of the CDs containing various concentrations of 4-NP (0–0.001 μM) solution (C) under UV light and (D) daylight.
(A) The absorption spectra of 2-NP and 4-NP and fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectral of CDs and CDs upon addition of 2-NP and 4-NP.
Synthetic route of CDs and process of the probe for detecting 2-NP and 4-NP by fluorescence and colorimetric process.

head.ce:title: The carbon dots as colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout probe for 2-nitrophenol and 4-nitrophenol detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Colleges of Heilongjiang Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Colleges of Heilongjiang Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis; College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis; College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has been synthesized by hydrothermal method by using ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a raw materials. The prepared CDs exhibited bright blue fluorscence excited by 340 nm with the quantum yield as high as 52%. The CDs possess special fluorescence responses to 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), and the slump of fluorescence was demonstrated by CDs solution in the case of 2-nitrophenol and 4-nitrophenol existence. The detection limit of 2-NP and 4-NP was calculated at 77 nM and 60 nM, respectively. Meanwhile, the color response to 2-NP and 4-NP can be observed from achromatic solution to yellow by naked eye. All in all, the obtained CDs can be applied as fluorescence and colorimetric probe in environmental protection.

Synthetic route of CDs and process of the probe for detecting 2-NP and 4-NP by fluorescence and colorimetric process.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots, known as carbon nanodots or carbon dots, are a new class of spherical carbon nanomaterials of a size less than 10 nm [1]. It is a kind of nanoclusters composed of very few molecules or atoms that can be dispersed in water or organic solvents to form colloid. In the last few years, the CDs became more popular, which is regarded as a new star of the “fluorescent material family”, and many research groups had studied it [2]. Since the Sun's pioneering work, fluorescent CDs have caught intensive attention for their charming features, including unique excitation-dependent fluorescence, easy preparation superior, photostability, excellent biocompatibility and high water-solubility [3–7]. Therefore, CDs can be widely used in catalysis, biological imaging, other fields, photovoltaic devices, MRI applications and medical diagnosis [8–19]. The CDs is also easy to achieve surface functionalization by chemical modification [20], so the CDs is the ideal material to replace the traditional quantum dots in biomarkers, fluorescence imaging and probe, due to CDs is a carbon-containing material that has no toxic effect on the organism. Therefore, the research scope of fluorescence CDs is more extensive. With the development of science and technology, there are more and more ways to prepare CDs. So far, countless techniques such as chemical oxidation, ultrasonic synthesis, electrochemical synthesis, laser ablation, microwave pyrolysis and hydrothermal carbonization have been reported for preparing fluorescent CDs [21–25]. However, most of them need expensive apparatuses and complex time-consuming processes [26].. Among them, one-pot hydrothermal approach is an attractive way to produce CDs, due to it is a simple cheap and sustainable method. Unfortunately, the fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of general CDs is relative low. So to fabricate CDs with ample quantum yields of photoluminescence, a surface-modifying procedure is required [27]. Up to now, a great deal of non-metal heteroatoms (such as N, S, B, or P) doped CDs have been synthesized [28]. Among them, the N atom doping the CDs have been proved improving the QY of CDs, due to the intrinsic electronic and surface properties of CDs can be optimized during the process of N-doping [29–35]..

In recent years, many literatures have reported the CDs can be treated as fluorescent sensor in hunting environmental pollutants. The fluorescent slumping behavior of CDs after combination with nitroaromatic or metal ions, which is the primary aspect to apply CDs as a fluorescent probe. As a kind of explosives nitroaromatic compounds, nitroaromatic are well-known harmful substance, they are seriously dangerous to both social safety and environment, so it is very important for their detection. Among them, 4-NP and 2-NP may exist in the water environment for a long period because of their solubility and stability. The acute inhalation or ingestion of 4-NP can cause liver, kidney damage, headaches, fervescence, methemoglobinemia and so on. Thus, 4-NP has been listed in “priority pollutant” by the U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [36]. Therefore, it is very important to detect and monitor nitroaromatic explosives in the environment in real time. The design and preparation of rapid, cheap, non-toxic, portable detection probes has important theoretical significance and practical application value.

In this work, the nitrogen-containing CDs with blue fluorescence were manufactured by single-step hydrothermal approach using EDTA·2Na as raw material. The prepared CDs possessed good optical properties, excellent water solubility and low toxicity. The CDs also had excellent stability at different pH, ionic strength and storage time. It can recognize 2-NP and 4-NP by fluorometric and colorimetric method quickly and effectively. Because of the specific fluorescence quenching and colorimetry of nitroaromatic by CDs, we can monitor their trace vividly and handy in water bodies, thus achieving effective treatment.

All chemical reagents used were of analytical grade or of the high purity available. Urea was obtained from Aladdin, Edetate disodium (EDTA·2Na) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Fenghua Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin). The used nitroaromatic 2, 4, 6-Trinitrophenol (TNP), nitrobenzene (NB), 3-Nitrotoluene (3-NT), 3-Nitrophenol (3-NP), o-Nitrophenol (2-NP), methylbenzene, 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP), 2, 4-Dinitrotoluene (DNT), 2, 4, 6-Trinitrotoluene (TNT) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai).

The CDs were produced by a one-step hydrothermal method. In detail, urea (2 g) and edetate disodium (1.52 g) were mixed and heated at 160 °C for 6 h under solvothermal condition in 10 mL DMF and then cooled to room temperature. In order to remove the superfluous liquid in the upper layer, the light yellow solution was then centrifuged at 12,000 r min−1 for 10 min, and the solid was freeze-dried to obtain the light color powder of CDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the CDs morphology and size were characterized with a JEOLFETEM-2100 transmission electron microscope operated at an accelerating volt-age of 200 kV. Fluorescence spectra were recorded by Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA) and a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer. XPS spectra measurements were measured on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60 S). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a JASCOFT/IR-420. UV–vis spectra were recorded on U-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensing for 2-NP and 4-NP
ce:section.ce:para: The 0.08 g CDs powder was dissolved in 3 mL pure water to form a CDs solution as detection probe. The obtained solution was used as fluorescent and colorimetric probe for sensing the 2-NP and 4-NP. The fluorescence emission of all specimens were measured at excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Then a 150 μL of 2-NP solution with concentration ranging from 0 to 1 mM was added and constant volume (150 μL) with CDs probe. In comparison, 150 μL of sample of nitroaromatic solution (10−3 M, including toluene, NB, 4-NP, 3-NP, 3-NT, DNT, TNT, TNP) was added to the CDs solution in the same conditions. When detecting for 4-NP, the processes was similar with 2-NP. After incubated for 3 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra of all samples were measured under the excitation of 340 nm. The UV–Vis spectra of all samples were also recorded in a wavelength range from 200 nm to 700 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, a kind of CDs based colorimetric and fluorescent probe was explored to recognize 2-NP and 4-NP. The synthesis and process of detection were illustrated by Scheme 1. Hydrothermal method provided a simple way for preparing the CDs. As a result, the CDs were obtained by thermal treatment of EDTA·2Na and urea in DMF at 160 °C for 6 h in high-pressure batch autoclave, and light yellow powder was acquired after freeze-drying. The as-synthesized CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV-lamp. As a dual readout probe, the CDs emission exhibited “turn off” response to the 2-NP and 4-NP, correspondingly, the colorimetric transformation colorless to yellow also manifested clearly.

In order to understand the structure and surface information of CDs, TEM, FT-IR and XPS were measured. The morphology and size of the CDs was provided by TEM that revealed spherical in shape and monodispersed states, the particle size distribution was ranged from 2.0 to 5.0 nm with an average diameter of 3.0 nm ( Fig. 1A). As displayed in Fig. 1A, the high-resolution TEM, most particles had a discernible lattice structure and the lattice spacing of ca. 0.34 nm, which attributed to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon.

To identify the surface functional groups on the CDs the FT-IR was recorded, and there are several absorption peaks with different absorption characteristics in the range of 1000–3500 cm−1. The CDs exhibited peaks at 3442 cm−1, corresponding to the O-H vibrations. And the absorption bands at 1668 cm−1 was corresponded to stretching vibration of C˭O, and bending and stretching vibrations of -CH3 can be observed at around 2975 cm−1. The peaks at 1633, 1392, 1150 cm−1 were related to C˭C, C-N, and N-H stretching vibrations. And the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C-O-C was located at 1020 cm−1.

The composition of CDs was checked by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full XPS spectrum ( Fig. 2A) showed three peaks at 285.98, 400.31 and 529.98 eV, which can be ascribed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. In addition, the high-resolution spectra of the characteristic atom were also displayed in Fig. B-D. The C1s exhibited four peaks at 284.38, 287.03, 287.11, and 289.78 eV respectively (Fig. 2B), due to the four types of carbon bonds (C-C/C˭C, C-N, C-O, and C˭O existed) [37]. And high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2D) demonstrated three characteristic peak located at 398.13, 399.92 and 401.22 eV, which was related to the N bond in a graphite-like structure (N-C3), pyridinic-like N, and N-H bond of NH2 groups, respectively [38]. The high-resolution of O1s had two peaks distributed at 532.01 and 529.92 eV, designated to C˭O and C-OH/C-O-C groups respectively. Thus, both FT-IR and XPS data confirmed that the surface CDs possessed rich O- and N-containing functional groups.

To study the CDs optical properties, the a FL spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra were tested and showed in Fig. 3A. There is a characteristic UV–vis absorption peak at 340 nm, which can be assigned to the n-π * transition of C˭O and π-π * transition of C˭C. And then absorbance peak manifested that there are a large numbers of polar functional groups on the surface of CDs [39]. When they were illuminated at UV light (340 nm), the CDs solution emitted bright blue fluorescence. The color of the CDs solution without and with UV raying can be noticed in the inset of right image. The photoluminescence spectra of the CDs excited at a wavelength range from 280 to 400 nm were also investigated (Fig. 3B). As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission peak was shifted to a higher wavelength, which can be manifested by an "edge redshift effect" [40].. The intensity of emission peak raised and then depressed with rising excitation wavelength. In contrast, 340 nm was pinpointed the best excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 3C, the decays were well fitted by double-exponential, indicating that the average lifetimes of 4.55 ns for CDs, respectively. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, it was found that the fluorescence quantum yield of CD was 52%. And the corresponding formula is filled with in the supporting information.

Considering that the complexity of the actual environment may interfere with the application of the CDs, the characteristic properties of the CDs under different conditions were tested. Firstly, the influence of ionic strength on fluorescence intensity was studied by dropping different concentrations of NaCl solution. As displayed in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was not changed virtually even the concentration of NaCl solution was reached to 1.0 M. Secondly, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs with the variation of time was also inspected. The variety of fluorescence intensity can be the neglected according to the Fig. 4B, which proved the stability of the CDs in the long-time storing. At last, the effect of pH value was explored by completive experiments. Learn from Fig. 4C, the stable fluorescence intensity of CDs persisted in a large range of pH 1–14, denoted that the CDs possessed the potential to work in both acidic and basic environments. Above results manifested that the CDs have good stability and possibility which allowed them can be applied to a number of tasks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of the CDs for 2-NP and 4-NP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The perfermance of detection was tested by monitoring the 2-NP and 4-NP. The fluorescent response of CDs was investigated in the existence of different nitroaromatic compounds (including toluene, 2-NP, NB, TNT, TNP, 4-NP, 3-NT, 3-NP and DNT) with the concentration of 100 μM under the same condition ( Fig. 5A). The fluorescence emission of CDs remained virtually even the concentration of the other samples was added up to 10−3 M apart from the existence of 2-NP and 4-NP. The 2-NP can quench the fluorescence emission greatly. Meanwhile the 4-NP with the emission of CDs plunged with the emission wavelength shift. It is implied that the emission of CDs solution is susceptible of 2-NP and 4-NP. Upon addition of 4-NP, the FL emission at 405 nm slumped and splitted two peaks located at 385 nm and 450 nm respectively. Therefore, the CDs were found to be essential detecting the 2-NP and 4-NP in environment. After exposed to 2-NP and 4-NP, the brightness of the probe in the UV lamp was significantly fainted, while the brightness of the CDs did not change significantly with other samples. Simultaneously, the color of CDs solution turned from a colorless to yellow with addition of 2-NP, 4-NP under daylight. However, there was almost no color change with other nitroaromatic compounds (Fig. 5C, D). As depicted in Fig. 5B, the UV–vis spectra manifested the colorimetric response to 2-NP and 4-NP compared with others. When 2-NP and 4-NP dropped into CDs probe, 2-NP and 4-NP trigger induce a noticeable spectral change and the absorption wavelength red-shifted. The 2-NP and 4-NP can lead to electron-transfer from the CDs to the 2-NP and 4-NP which resulted in the fluorescence quenching and the variation in color [41,42]. The fluorescent and colorimetric response of the CDs reach a consensus exactly in presence of 2-NP and 4-NP. Thus, except as a fluorescent sensor, these results implied that CDs could act as a potential candidate for a naked-eyes colorimetric sensor for 2-NP and 4-NP [43].

For all the specify of CDs to 2-NP and 4-NP was highlighted, the interference experiments of common nitroaromatic compounds on the relative FL intensities of CDs under co-existent conditions was also performed ( Fig. 6). All tests were performed at competitive quenching conditions with analyte to disturbing nitroaromatic ration of compounds with the concentration and amount of 1:1. The fluorescence intensity (I/I0) in the presence of other nitroaromatic compounds co-existing with 2-NP was provided respectively, the comparison indicated that other compounds had no interference with the CDs detecting 2-NP except 4-NP (Fig. 6A). Similarly, the detection for 4-NP has also taken on excellent ability of anti-interference (Fig. 6C). The CDs in the UV light and sunlight also clearly showed that the fluorescence brightness and color of the CDs had not changed significantly when exposed to other nitroaromatic compounds (Fig. 6B, D). Apart from their mutual effect, the CDs can be treated as a superb dual signal sensor for 2-NP and 4-NP.

At the same condition, the capability of this probe for quantitative detecting for 2-NP was assessed. On the one hand, the fluorescence spectra of CDs in variations concentrations of 2-NP solution were recorded and displayed in Fig. 7. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs slumped gradually with the rising of 2-NP concentration (Fig. 7A). When the concentration of 2-NP reached 10−4 M, the fluorescence emission peak became split and divided into two peaks. When the concentration of 2-NP reached 1 mM, the FL intensity was exhausted completely. Simultaneouly, the higher the concentration of 2-NP, the lower the brightness of the CDs, which can be seen evidently by naked eye with the UV lamp. When the concentration was 1 mM, the brightness of the CDs was almost quenched (Fig. 7C). Notably, the ratio of fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of 2-NP displays a linear response to the 2-NP concentration in the range of 0.001–1 μM, the corresponding linear equation was I/I0 = -0.1194 × +0.9813 (R2 = 0.98) in Fig. 7A inset. (I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of 2-NP, respectively). On the other hand, as the concentration of 2-NP raised, the probe discolored from colorless to yellow (Fig. 7D). The color change was caused by 2-NP can be recorded by UV–visible spectrophotometry (Fig. 7B). The absorption peaks were produced at 290 and 425 nm, and the absorption became higher and wider. Correspondly, the color deepen gradualy with the rising of 2-NP. The results indicated that the proposed dual functional sensor might be a suitable tool for the detection of 2-NP, which provided two different and connected ways to discriminate 2-NP.

Similar behavior is observed in the curves for the 4-NP detection ( Fig. 8A). With the addition of 4-NP, the fluorescence curve showed a continuous decrease, which indicated an intense fluorescent response. When the consistency of 4-NP reached 10−4 M, the fluorescence emission peak gradually split two independent peaks located at 383 nm and 484 nm, respectively. And when the amount of 4-NP extend to 10−3 M, the fluorescence emission of the CDs produced displacement at 455 nm. Under UV lamp in Fig. 8C, the brightness of CDs solution fainted gradually with the boost of 4-NP concentration. A good linear correlation (linear equation: I/I0 =−0.1392x+ 0.9318 (R2 =0.99)) was obtained over the concentration range of 0.001–1 μM (Fig. 8A inset). These results demonstrated that fluorescence emission of CDs were very susceptible to 4-NP. Similarly, the absorption of samples illustrated the same results, with the increasing of 4-NP concentration, a new absorption peak emerged at 402 nm and the peak gradually widens (Fig. 8B). As depicted in Fig. 8D, the CDs solution displayed a highly selective colorimetric response, with the addition of 4-NP concentration, the color of CDs transformed from colorless to yellow. Notably, though the colorimetric response is equal, the absorptions exhibited great distinction,which allowed the CDs to recognize them (2-NP and 4-NP) each other easily. From this point of view, the CDs solution can quickly and effectively detect 2-NP and 4NP by both colorimetric and fluorenscent methods.

To better comprehend the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the UV–Vis absorption response to the analyte were further analyzed. The fluorescence quenching of CDs after exposing 2-NP and 4-NP may be caused by the inner filter effect (IFE) [34]. The spectral overlap between the absorption, and the excitation and/or emission band can enhance the IFE of the fluorophore [44]. Since the absorption band of 2-NP and 4-NP was almost completely covered by the excitation and emission band of the CDs, which was in favor of energry transfer ( Fig. 9). As a consequence, 2-NP and 4-NP can not only shelter the excitation light of the CDs, but also assimilate the emission light from CDs. Thus, the absorbance enhancement of 2-NP and 4-NP could be alternated to fluorescence quenching of CDs. In addition, the discolor of CDs solution may be caused by valence electron transition. The UV–vis spectrum of CDs exhibited a weak absorbance peak at the 340 nm and the absorption peak was not absorbed at 390–430 nm (Fig. 9B). Whereas broad absorption (CDs+2-NP and CDs+4-NP) was observed for the wavelength 390–430 nm, corresponding, the color of samples transformed from colorless to light yellow which was consistent with absorption. The possible mechanism provided reasonable theoretical supporticy for the detection.

In a word, we described a simple method to prepared fluorescence CDs. The quantum yield of as-syntesized CDs was as high as 52%. The CDs featured stability in long rang of pH, high ionic strength and long storage time make CDs hopeful in more application. Rated as a convenient and low toxicity sensor, the CDs exhibited intense colorimetric and fluoresent response to 2-NP and 4-NP. A good linearity for detecting 2-NP was acquired over the concentration range from 0.001 to 1 μM with a detection limit of 77 nM, while the concentrations of 4-NP ranging from 0.001 to 1 μM with a detection limit of 60 nM displayed a linear relationship. These results demonstrated that CDs can act as smart fluorescent and colorimetric probes to detect 2-NP and 4-NP in the environment. Therefore, this work provided a simple, easy to operate method, which can provide a cost-effective, widely used and high-quality CDs for environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Fund (LBH-Q17084), Higher Education Teaching Reform Research of Heilongjiang Province (SJGY20170179) and Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201801).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.12.017.
